abstract:
Sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) have emerged as a frontrunner for sustainable energy storage, yet the development of high-energy-density cathodes remains hindered by structural strain during the (de)sodiation process. In this work, we present a P2-layered oxide cathode, $\text{Na}_{0.7}\text{Li}_{0.03}[\text{Mg}_{0.15}\text{Li}_{0.07}\text{Mn}_{0.75}]\text{O}_2$ (NMLMO), featuring a unique lithium dual-site substitution strategy where Li ions occupy both transition-metal (TM) and alkali-metal (AM) layers. 

Through a synergy of theoretical modeling and experimental analysis, we show that Li on the TM sites creates $\text{Na–O–Li}$ electronic configurations that activate and enhance capacity through oxygen anionic redox. Simultaneously, Li ions in the AM layers act as rigid $\text{LiO}_6$ prismatic pillars, effectively pinning the layered framework to suppress detrimental phase transitions (such as P2-OP4 and P2-P’2). Consequently, NMLMO delivers a high specific capacity of $266\text{ mAh g}^{-1}$ while maintaining a "nearly zero-strain" state (only 1.2% volume variation) across a broad voltage window of 1.5–4.6 V. These results demonstrate that dual-site substitution is a powerful tool for overcoming the traditional trade-off between high capacity and structural stability in advanced rechargeable batteries.
